promptDef.name: angular.subscription-management.unsafe

assessments.name: Subscription Management
assessments.description: Make sure to to ALWAYS unsubscribe from observables. Use takeUntilDestroyed(), take(), takeUntil() or async pipe - avoid manual unsubscribe() calls.

outputFiles.code:
{{ (product$ | async)?.name }}

{{ (product$ | async)?.description }}

{{ (product$ | async)?.price }}

attemptDetails.reasoning:
We are given a violation of the rule "angular-subscription-management-unsafe". The rule: we must fix the unsafe subscription by using AsyncPipe. Steps to fix: 1. Remove the manual subscription (in ngOnInit, the subscribe call). 2. Expose the observable as a class property (e.g., `product$ = this.productService.getProduct()`). 3. Add AsyncPipe to the component's imports (and import AsyncPipe from '@angular/common'). 4. Update the template to use the async pipe (and optional chaining because the value might be null until the async data arrives). 5. Tidy the class: remove the `product` property and the `ngOnInit` since it's now empty (and also remove `implements OnInit`). Important: We must fix both the .ts file and the .html file (or the inline template if applicable). Looking at the provided files: File: src/app/app.ts (at location 17-17, which is the line with the subscribe call) Current code in app.ts: export class App implements OnInit { private productService = inject(ProductService); product: Product | null = null; ngOnInit(): void { this.productService.getProduct().subscribe((data) => { this.product = data; }); } } We are going to: - Remove the `product` property and the `ngOnInit` method. - Create an observable property `product$` that holds the result of `this.productService.getProduct()`. - Add `AsyncPipe` to the component's `imports` and import it. Also, we have to update the template (app.html) to replace every occurrence of `product` with `(product$ | async)` and use optional chaining because the observable might emit null. Current app.html:
... [attr.src]="product?.image" ... [alt]="product?.name" ...

{{ product?.name }}

{{ product?.description }}

{{ product?.price }}
We change to: ... [attr.src]="(product$ | async)?.image" ... [alt]="(product$ | async)?.name" ...

{{ (product$ | async)?.name }}

... and similarly for description and price. Additionally, note that the rule example had a typo (lenght) but we are not introducing typos. We must be cautious about the original code. However, note the rule checklist: "Component still compiles and the UI shows the same data (fix any typo like `lenght` → `length` in the template)." In this case, there are no typos (like "lenght") in the provided template. Steps for the .ts file: - Import AsyncPipe from '@angular/common' - Add to the component decorator: `imports: [AsyncPipe]` - Change the class: Remove `product: ...` and `ngOnInit` Add `product$ = this.productService.getProduct();` - Also, note that the class name is `App`. We might want to change it to `AppComponent` but the rule doesn't require that unless it's necessary for the refactoring? Actually, the rule example did change to `UserComponent` but that was just an example. We should preserve the class name unless we have a good reason? The rule says: preserve existing behavior. So we leave the class name as `App`. However, note: the rule example had a class name change. But in the provided code, the class name is `App`. We are not required to change the class name unless it's part of the rule? The rule doesn't say so. So we keep `App`. But wait: the rule example changed the class name for some reason? Actually, it was just an example. We are to fix the violation without changing behavior. So we leave the class name. Steps for the .html file: Replace every `product` with `(product$ | async)` and wrap the access in optional chaining (so `product?.name` becomes `(product$ | async)?.name`). However, note that the rule example did: `{{ (items$ | async)?.lenght }}` but we are fixing to `length`? Actually, in our case the property is `name`, `description`, `price` and they are exactly as they are. Important: The template uses `[attr.src]="product?.image"` and `[alt]="product?.name"`. We change these to: [attr.src]="(product$ | async)?.image" [alt]="(product$ | async)?.name" And similarly, the interpolation is `{{ product?.name }}` becomes `{{ (product$ | async)?.name }}` But note: the interpolation might be inside double quotes? Actually, in the template it's written as `{{ ... }}`. So we change the inside. However, note that the rule example had a backslash in `{{ product?.name \}}` - that's because in markdown we escape braces. The actual code doesn't have backslashes. So in the code we write without backslashes. Let's write the corrected template. Also note: the rule example had a typo in the TO: "lenght" instead of "length", but we are not changing the existing product property names. We are replacing: product?.name -> (product$ | async)?.name product?.description -> (product$ | async)?.description product?.price -> (product$ | async)?.price product?.image -> (product$ | async)?.image But note: the `product` in the template is sometimes used as `product` (without optional chaining) but the original code uses `product?....` which is safe because the initial value is null. However, the async pipe will emit null first (or maybe undefined) until the data arrives. So we keep the optional chaining. Actually, the async pipe will emit null initially because the Product type might be `Product | null`. So we need the optional chaining. Implementation: We are going to generate two fixed files: app.ts (with the changes) and app.html (with the changes). Let's write the fixed app.ts: Before: import { ChangeDetectionStrategy, Component, OnInit } from "@angular/core"; import { Product } from "./product.model"; import { ProductService } from "./product.service"; @Component({ selector: "app-root", templateUrl: "./app.html", }) export class App implements OnInit { private productService = inject(ProductService); product: Product | null = null; ngOnInit(): void { this.productService.getProduct().subscribe((data) => { this.product = data; }); } } After: import { ChangeDetectionStrategy, Component, inject } from "@angular/core"; // note: we remove OnInit from the import because we no longer use it, but the rule says to remove the `implements OnInit` and the empty ngOnInit. // We add import for AsyncPipe import { AsyncPipe } from "@angular/common"; import { Product } from "./product.model"; import { ProductService } from "./product.service"; @Component({ selector: "app-root", templateUrl: "./app.html", imports: [AsyncPipe], // added AsyncPipe to imports }) export class App { private productService = inject(ProductService); product$ = this.productService.getProduct(); // replaces the product property and the subscription } We removed: implements OnInit ngOnInit method the product property We added: import { AsyncPipe } from "@angular/common"; imports: [AsyncPipe] in the component decorator Note: We changed `inject` to be used without the type parameters?
